verdict_note: 5/6 P1 fixed (collision, disconnectAll, removeServer, aggregateTools error handling, unsafe cast). Remaining P1 (orphan code) is task-scope appropriate — integration in subsequent tasks.

findings.title: Package has no consumer -- mcp-hub is still orphan code
findings.code_snippet:
export { McpHub } from './mcp-hub'
export type { ConnectEnabledResult, ServerStatus } from './mcp-hub'
export { McpClientManager } from './mcp-client-manager'
export { McpServerStore } from './server-store'
export { createTransport } from './transport-factory'
findings.description: No package in the monorepo depends on @proj-airi/mcp-hub. A grep for '@proj-airi/mcp-hub' across all package.json files returns only the package's own package.json. The exports are not imported by any app (stage-tamagotchi, stage-web, server) or other package. This was flagged in iter1 as review-code-002 and remains unresolved. Per CLAUDE.md orphan detection rules, code without a live entry point is dead-on-arrival.
findings.suggestion: Wire mcp-hub into at least one consumer before merging. For example: (1) Add @proj-airi/mcp-hub as a dependency of apps/stage-tamagotchi or apps/server. (2) Import McpHub in the Anima orchestrator or a new IPC handler. (3) Provide a minimal end-to-end path: UI config -> IPC -> McpHub.addServer() -> McpHub.connectServer() -> tools available to LLM. Even a single thin integration proves the package is reachable.

findings.title: aggregateTools collision handling is asymmetric -- first tool keeps short name
findings.description: When two servers expose a tool with the same name (e.g., 'search'), the first server's tool is stored under the plain name 'search', while the second server's tool is stored under 'serverB__search'. The first server's tool is never retroactively namespaced. This creates an asymmetric API: callers see 'search' (from server A) and 'serverB__search' (from server B) but have no way to discover which server owns the un-namespaced 'search'. With 3+ servers sharing a name, only the first keeps the short key. This is a correctness issue: the behavior depends on insertion order into the clients Map and is non-obvious to consumers.
findings.suggestion: When a collision is detected, retroactively namespace the FIRST tool as well. Implementation: (1) Track tool origins: Map<toolName, serverId>. (2) On collision, rename the existing entry from 'toolName' to 'originalServerId__toolName', then add 'newServerId__toolName'. This ensures all colliding tools are namespaced symmetrically. Alternatively, always namespace all tools (simpler, more predictable).

findings.title: disconnectAll silently swallows failures after clearing clients map
findings.description: When disconnectAll() encounters failures, it detects them (line 48-49) but only calls this.clients.clear() to force-clean the map. The failure reasons are never logged, never thrown, and never returned to the caller. The caller (McpHub.shutdown) has no way to know that some MCP server connections may still be alive as zombie child processes. This is especially problematic for stdio transports where a failed close means the child process is still running.

findings.title: removeServer catch block silently swallows disconnect errors
findings.code_snippet:
try { await this.clientManager.disconnect(id) }
catch { /* proceed with removal even if disconnect fails */ }
findings.description: The catch block in removeServer has a comment explaining intent (proceed with removal) but the error parameter is not even bound. This means: (1) No logging of the failed disconnect -- operators cannot diagnose why the disconnect failed, (2) For stdio transports, a failed disconnect could leave a zombie child process, (3) The comment documents intent but does not constitute handling. Per CLAUDE.md error_handling rules: 'Catch -> Log with context -> Re-throw typed error or handle gracefully'. A bare catch with only a comment violates this.

findings.title: aggregateTools loop has no error handling for individual server failures
findings.description: The aggregateTools method iterates over all connected clients and calls client.tools() sequentially. If any single server fails (network timeout, process crash, protocol error), the entire aggregation aborts with an unhandled exception. This means: (1) One flaky server prevents tool discovery from all servers, (2) The caller (McpHub.aggregateTools) has no per-server error granularity, (3) For a hub managing multiple servers, this is a single-point-of-failure pattern. This is a network I/O operation without try/catch protection.

findings.title: Unsafe `as McpServerConfig` cast in update() breaks union safety
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 6/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 4/10 (Aggregate: 5.75). The spread of `Partial<McpServerConfigInput>` onto an existing `McpServerConfig` produces an untyped object that is force-cast with `as McpServerConfig`. Because McpServerConfig is a discriminated union, the compiler cannot verify the merged result is a valid variant. For example, spreading `{ transport: 'sse', url: '...' }` onto a `StdioServerConfig` would produce an object with both `command` and `url` fields -- an illegal state the union is specifically designed to prevent. The subsequent `validateConfig(merged)` catches some issues at runtime, but the cast bypasses the type system's compile-time protection entirely.
findings.suggestion: Instead of spreading and casting, reconstruct the config through `buildConfig()` using the merged input values. Parse the merged partial back into a `McpServerConfigInput` first, then call `buildConfig(base, mergedInput)` to get a properly typed `McpServerConfig`. This ensures the discriminated union is constructed through a validated code path.

findings.title: No structured logging anywhere in the package
findings.description: The entire mcp-hub package has zero logging. Server connections, disconnections, failures, tool aggregation, config changes -- none are logged. This was flagged in iter1 (review-code-007) and remains unresolved. Per CLAUDE.md, structured logging with @guiiai/logg is the standard pattern across this monorepo. MCP server lifecycle events are operational data that will be needed for production debugging. The silent catch blocks in removeServer (line 41) and transport cleanup (mcp-client-manager.ts line 28) make this especially important -- errors are being swallowed with no observability.
findings.suggestion: Inject @guiiai/logg via useLogg() pattern. Log at INFO level: server added/removed/updated, connection established/closed, tool aggregation results. Log at WARN level: connection failures (in connectEnabled failed array), tool name collisions. Log at ERROR level: store corruption, transport creation failures. Include serverId, serverName, and duration_ms in structured fields. Replace the /* best-effort cleanup */ and /* proceed with removal */ comments with actual logger.warn() calls.

findings.title: McpClientManager.getTools() error path untested
findings.description: The getTools() method throws when called with a non-connected server ID, but no test exercises this error path. The manager test file only tests getTools() on connected servers (line 48 of mcp-client-manager.test.ts). This leaves the error branch on line 65 unverified.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: `it('throws when getting tools for non-connected server', async () => { await expect(manager.getTools('non-existent')).rejects.toThrow('Server not connected: non-existent') })`

findings.title: Tool name collision/namespacing in aggregateTools() untested
findings.description: The aggregateTools() method has a collision-handling branch: when two servers expose a tool with the same name, the second gets namespaced as `${serverId}__${toolName}`. This branch is never exercised because the test suite only connects one server at a time. This is non-trivial business logic -- the collision handling has a subtle behavior where the first tool keeps its short name while only the second gets namespaced, which could confuse consumers.
findings.suggestion: Add a test with two connected MCP servers that both expose an 'echo' tool. Verify that one keeps the name 'echo' and the other gets namespaced as '<serverId>__echo'. This requires either a second fixture server or reusing the same fixture with a different config entry. The test proves the collision logic works correctly.

findings.title: McpHub.connectServer() error path for non-existent ID untested
findings.description: McpHub.connectServer() throws when the server ID does not exist in the store. This error branch is not covered by any test in mcp-hub.test.ts. While the store-level tests cover getById returning undefined, the hub-level error path needs its own verification.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: `it('throws when connecting non-existent server', async () => { await expect(hub.connectServer('non-existent')).rejects.toThrow('MCP server not found: non-existent') })`

findings.title: McpHub.connectEnabled() return value not asserted
findings.description: The connectEnabled() method returns a ConnectEnabledResult with connected[] and failed[] arrays, but the test discards this return value. The test only checks statuses via getServerStatus(). The ConnectEnabledResult structure is part of the public API (exported in index.ts) and should be verified to ensure callers get accurate reporting.
findings.suggestion: Capture the return value: `const result = await hub.connectEnabled()` and assert `expect(result.connected).toContain(enabledConfig.id)` and `expect(result.failed).toEqual([])`.

findings.title: McpClientManager.disconnectAll() failure handling untested
findings.description: The disconnectAll() method has specific failure-handling logic: when any disconnect fails, it clears the entire clients map. This important cleanup behavior is never tested. The afterEach hooks call disconnectAll() but only in the success path.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that forces a disconnect failure (e.g., by manually corrupting the client map or testing with an already-closed transport) and verifies that clients are cleared after failure. This ensures the recovery logic works.

findings.title: McpHub.updateServer() and McpHub.getServer() untested at hub level
findings.description: Two public methods on McpHub (getServer and updateServer) have no test coverage in mcp-hub.test.ts. While the underlying store methods are tested in server-store.test.ts, the hub-level methods are part of the public API and could diverge from the store behavior (e.g., if hub adds connection-aware logic in the future). At minimum, a smoke test verifying delegation is warranted.
findings.suggestion: Add hub-level tests: (1) `hub.addServer(...)` then `hub.getServer(id)` returns the config, (2) `hub.updateServer(id, { name: 'new' })` updates and returns the modified config. These are quick to write and protect against future regressions when hub-level logic is added.

findings.title: Transport cleanup catch block lacks error binding and logging
findings.code_snippet:
try { await (transport as { close: () => Promise<void> }).close() }
catch { /* best-effort cleanup */ }
findings.description: Inside the connect() error path, when the MCP client creation fails, the code attempts to close the transport as best-effort cleanup. The catch block swallows the cleanup error entirely without binding the error parameter or logging. While best-effort cleanup is a reasonable pattern, silently swallowing the cleanup error means: (1) If the transport is a stdio process, a failed close could leave a zombie process with no diagnostic trail, (2) The outer catch on line 30 will throw the original error, so the cleanup failure is completely invisible. This is not a P0 (it's a cleanup path, not primary logic) but warrants at least a debug-level log.

findings.title: SSE/HTTP URL validation only checks non-empty, not valid URL format
findings.description: For SSE and HTTP transports, the URL is only validated for truthiness (non-empty). Any non-empty string passes validation, including 'not-a-url', 'javascript:alert(1)', or relative paths like '../../../etc/passwd'. While this won't cause command injection (unlike stdio), it means invalid URLs are persisted to SQLite and the error only surfaces later when createMCPClient tries to connect -- producing an obscure network error instead of a clear validation error at add/update time. The command allowlist for stdio was well-implemented; URL validation should match that level of rigor.
findings.suggestion: Add URL format validation: `try { new URL(url) } catch { throw new Error('Invalid URL for ${transport} transport: ${url}') }`. Optionally restrict to http/https protocols: `if (!['http:', 'https:'].includes(new URL(url).protocol)) throw new Error(...)`. This catches malformed URLs at the boundary (add/update) rather than at connection time.

findings.title: createTransport has no error handling for StdioMCPTransport construction
findings.description: The createTransport factory function constructs an Experimental_StdioMCPTransport without any error handling. If the constructor throws (e.g., command not found on PATH, permission denied, invalid args format), the raw library error propagates with no additional context about which MCP server config caused the failure. The caller (McpClientManager.connect) does wrap this in a try/catch, but the error message from the SDK constructor may lack the server ID/name context needed for debugging. This is mitigated by the fact that connect() wraps it with config.id in the error message, making this a P2 rather than P1.

findings.title: McpTransport union mixes class instance with plain objects
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.0). The McpTransport union mixes a class instance (Experimental_StdioMCPTransport) that lacks a `type` discriminant with plain objects that have `type: 'sse' | 'http'`. This makes type narrowing impossible via a single discriminant field. Consumers must use `instanceof` for the stdio variant and property checks for others. This forces the `transport as McpTransport` cast at line 21 of mcp-client-manager.ts because the SDK's createMCPClient cannot accept the heterogeneous union directly.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the stdio transport in an object with a `type: 'stdio'` discriminant, e.g., `{ type: 'stdio'; transport: Experimental_StdioMCPTransport }`. This makes the union consistently discriminated on `type` and eliminates the need for `as` casts when passing to the SDK. Alternatively, since `createTransport` is only consumed by `McpClientManager.connect()`, make it return the SDK-expected type directly and remove the exported `McpTransport` union.

findings.title: getTools/aggregateTools return Record<string, unknown> losing type info
findings.description: Encapsulation: 8/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 4/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.0). Both `getTools()` and `aggregateTools()` return `Record<string, unknown>`, discarding the SDK's tool definition type information. The `@ai-sdk/mcp` client's `.tools()` method returns typed tool definitions that include parameter schemas, descriptions, and execute functions. Collapsing this to `unknown` forces every consumer to perform unsafe casts or runtime checks to use the tools, defeating the purpose of the typed SDK.
findings.suggestion: Use the SDK's return type: `type McpTools = Awaited<ReturnType<McpClient['tools']>>` and use `McpTools` as the return type for both methods. This preserves the full tool definition types through the hub facade and allows consumers to use tools without casting.

findings.title: No Valibot schema validation at Imperative Shell boundary
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 6/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 6.25). The project standard is Valibot for validation (per CLAUDE.md), but McpServerStore uses a hand-rolled `validateConfig()` function. While the manual validation covers basic cases (empty name, missing command/url, shell metacharacter injection), it lacks: URL format validation for SSE/HTTP transports, header key/value sanitization, and name length limits. A Valibot schema would provide declarative, composable validation that is easier to maintain and test, and would produce consistent error formats across the codebase.
findings.suggestion: Define Valibot schemas for McpServerConfigInput variants (e.g., `StdioServerConfigInputSchema = v.object({ transport: v.literal('stdio'), command: v.pipe(v.string(), ...), ... })`). Use `v.union([StdioSchema, SseSchema, HttpSchema])` as the discriminated validator. The command allowlist and shell metachar checks can be custom Valibot validators. This aligns with the project's validation standard and makes the constraints declarative.

findings.title: Missing JSDoc on exported McpHub class (primary public API)
findings.code_snippet: export class McpHub {
findings.description: McpHub is the primary facade exported by this package. It has no JSDoc documenting its purpose, constructor parameters (dbPath), or lifecycle (shutdown). As the main entry point consumers interact with, this class especially benefits from documentation covering: what it does, that dbPath creates/opens a SQLite database, and that shutdown() must be called for cleanup.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the class and its public methods. At minimum: class-level description, constructor param documentation, and a note about shutdown() lifecycle requirement.

findings.title: Missing JSDoc on exported McpServerStore class
findings.code_snippet: export class McpServerStore {
findings.description: McpServerStore is exported and manages SQLite-backed MCP server configuration persistence. It has no JSDoc on the class or any of its public methods (add, getAll, getById, getEnabled, update, remove, close). Key details worth documenting: the constructor opens a SQLite database and creates tables, command allowlist validation is enforced, and close() must be called for cleanup.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the class and its public methods. Document the constructor's dbPath parameter, the command allowlist security model, and the close() lifecycle requirement.

findings.title: Missing JSDoc on exported McpClientManager class
findings.code_snippet: export class McpClientManager {
findings.description: McpClientManager is exported and manages MCP client connections and tool aggregation. It has no JSDoc on the class or its public methods (connect, disconnect, disconnectAll, isConnected, getConnectedIds, getTools, aggregateTools). The aggregateTools method has a non-obvious namespacing behavior for duplicate tool names that especially warrants documentation.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the class and its public methods. The aggregateTools() method should document the collision-avoidance strategy: duplicate tool names get namespaced as 'serverId__toolName'.

findings.title: Missing JSDoc on exported createTransport function
findings.code_snippet: export function createTransport(config: McpServerConfig): McpTransport {
findings.description: createTransport is an exported factory function with no JSDoc. It has an important behavioral distinction worth documenting: stdio transport creates a real Experimental_StdioMCPTransport instance that spawns a subprocess, while sse/http return plain config objects (not actual transport instances). This asymmetry could surprise consumers.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc documenting the function's purpose and the behavioral difference between stdio (returns live transport instance) and sse/http (returns config object to be consumed by createMCPClient).

findings.title: sse/http cases duplicated in safeRowToConfig, buildConfig, validateConfig
findings.description: The 'sse' and 'http' transport cases are structurally identical across three functions: safeRowToConfig (lines 195-208), buildConfig (lines 243-245), and validateConfig (lines 275-288). In each function the two cases produce the same logic with only the transport literal differing. This amounts to ~20 duplicated lines spread across the file. Since both SseServerConfig and HttpServerConfig share the same fields (url + headers), they can be collapsed into fall-through cases or a shared helper.
findings.suggestion:
Use fall-through cases:

BEFORE:
case 'sse': return { ...base, transport: 'sse', url: ... }
case 'http': return { ...base, transport: 'http', url: ... }

AFTER:
case 'sse':
case 'http': return { ...base, transport: row.transport, url: ... } as McpServerConfig

The transport-factory.ts already uses this fall-through pattern proving the approach is idiomatic in this codebase.

findings.title: SseServerConfig and HttpServerConfig types are structurally identical
findings.description: SseServerConfig and HttpServerConfig (and their Input counterparts) have identical fields aside from the transport discriminant literal. This structural duplication is the root cause of the repeated switch-case duplication in server-store.ts. A shared generic or base interface for URL-based transports would remove the need for separate cases throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Transport factory returns heterogeneous union with plain objects for SSE/HTTP
findings.description: The McpTransport type is a union of a class instance (StdioMCPTransport) and two plain object literals for SSE and HTTP. The plain objects lack the lifecycle methods (start/close) that the StdioMCPTransport class has. In mcp-client-manager.ts line 21, the transport is cast as `transport as McpTransport` when passed to createMCPClient, suggesting a type mismatch. If @ai-sdk/mcp's createMCPClient accepts these plain objects for SSE/HTTP, a brief comment documenting this would prevent future confusion.
findings.suggestion: Verify whether @ai-sdk/mcp's createMCPClient accepts plain {type, url, headers} objects for SSE/HTTP transports by checking the SDK documentation or source. If confirmed, add a comment: `// @ai-sdk/mcp accepts plain config objects for SSE/HTTP transports`. If not confirmed, use the appropriate SDK transport classes.

findings.title: Echo fixture server tools ignore input parameters
findings.description: Both fixture tools ignore their input parameters and return hardcoded values. The 'echo' tool always returns 'Echo: hello' regardless of input, and 'add' always returns '5'. This prevents tests from verifying that tool invocations pass arguments correctly through the MCP transport layer.
findings.suggestion: Enhance the fixture to accept and process parameters, then add a test that invokes the tool and verifies the response matches the input.

findings.title: connectEnabled could use Result pattern for type-safe error reporting
findings.description: The connectEnabled method returns a ConnectEnabledResult with connected/failed arrays. The failed array uses { id: string; error: string } which loses the original Error object (including cause chain). The current implementation is functional but could be improved by preserving error instances for programmatic inspection by callers.
findings.suggestion: Consider preserving the Error instance in the failed array: `failed: Array<{ id: string; error: string; cause?: unknown }>`. Or use a discriminated union Result type for each connection attempt.

findings.title: McpServerConfig interfaces lack readonly modifiers
findings.code_snippet:
interface McpServerConfigBase {
  id: string
  name: string
  enabled: boolean
  createdAt: number
  updatedAt: number
}
findings.description: McpServerConfigBase and all derived interfaces use mutable fields. Since these represent persisted configuration read from the database, they should be treated as immutable values -- mutations should only occur through the store's update() method. Adding `readonly` would prevent accidental mutation by consumers.
findings.suggestion: Mark all fields as `readonly`, or wrap the exported type: `export type McpServerConfig = Readonly<StdioServerConfig> | Readonly<SseServerConfig> | Readonly<HttpServerConfig>`.

findings.title: Verbose type assertions for transport cleanup in catch block
findings.description: The transport cleanup in the catch block uses three chained conditions and two type assertions to call close(). The repeated type assertions reduce readability.

findings.title: aggregateTools collision logic is non-obvious without comment
findings.description: The collision-handling strategy in aggregateTools is subtle: if a toolName already exists, the new tool is stored under a namespaced key, but the original tool retains the un-namespaced name. This asymmetry is non-obvious and a reader might expect both to be namespaced on collision.
findings.suggestion: Add a clarifying comment: `// First-come wins the short name; subsequent duplicates get server-prefixed names`

findings.title: validateConfig uses 'in' checks unnecessarily for discriminated union
findings.description: Inside the switch on input.transport, TypeScript already narrows the union to the stdio variant, which is guaranteed to have 'command' and optional 'args'. The 'in' guards are redundant since the discriminant has already narrowed the type.
findings.suggestion:
Use destructuring instead:

BEFORE: const command = 'command' in input ? input.command : undefined
AFTER: const { command, args } = input

positive_observations: Excellent iter1 fix: Command injection vulnerability resolved with ALLOWED_COMMANDS allowlist and SHELL_METACHAR_PATTERN regex in server-store.ts -- well-implemented security boundary with tests proving both allow and reject paths
Excellent iter1 fix: connectEnabled() now uses Promise.allSettled with ConnectEnabledResult return type, properly handling partial failures and reporting which servers succeeded vs failed
Excellent iter1 fix: update() now calls validateConfig(merged) after merge, closing the validation bypass gap
Excellent iter1 fix: safeJsonParse() wraps JSON.parse with try-catch and context-rich error message, protecting against corrupt database rows
Excellent iter1 fix: aggregateTools() now detects tool name collisions and namespaces the second occurrence
Server config persisted in SQLite with WAL mode -- correct 12-factor stateless pattern
All tests use real SQLite (temp files) and real stdio MCP server fixture (echo-mcp-server.ts) -- no mocks, no InMemoryRepository, no jest.fn(). Strong adherence to testing rules
Clean SRP separation: types.ts, server-store.ts, transport-factory.ts, mcp-client-manager.ts, mcp-hub.ts each have a single clear responsibility
Parameterized queries throughout server-store.ts with zero SQL string concatenation
Good persistence tests proving SQLite round-trip survives close/reopen, including the McpHub end-to-end persistence test
Zero mock violations: no jest.fn(), vi.fn(), vi.mock(), InMemoryRepository, MockXxx, or FakeXxx patterns anywhere in the test suite
Excellent Dev/Prod Parity: server-store.test.ts uses real SQLite (better-sqlite3) with temp directories, not in-memory substitutes
Real integration tests: mcp-client-manager.test.ts and mcp-hub.test.ts spawn a real MCP server process (echo-mcp-server.ts) over stdio transport
Strong persistence testing: both server-store and mcp-hub verify data survives close/reopen cycles
Security tests cover command allowlist enforcement, shell metacharacter rejection, and validation on both add and update paths
Test fixture uses the real @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, not a mock implementation
Constructor resource cleanup: McpServerStore constructor properly closes the database in the catch block if init fails, preventing resource leaks
Shutdown uses try/finally: McpHub.shutdown() properly uses try/finally to ensure store.close() runs even if disconnectAll() throws
Error wrapping with cause: McpClientManager.connect() wraps errors with new Error(msg, { cause: err }), preserving the original error chain

McpServerConfig is now a proper discriminated union on `transport` field with StdioServerConfig, SseServerConfig, and HttpServerConfig variants -- a significant improvement from iter1's flat interface
safeRowToConfig() uses a proper isTransportType() type guard and exhaustive switch instead of unsafe `as` cast
McpClientManager has good encapsulation with private Map and proper cleanup in disconnectAll() using Promise.allSettled
No forbidden patterns (TODO/FIXME/HACK/PLACEHOLDER) found anywhere in the codebase
Code is generally self-documenting with clear naming conventions, reducing the need for inline comments
Functions are consistently short (all under 30 lines) with early returns and guard clauses
Good use of named constants (ALLOWED_COMMANDS, SHELL_METACHAR_PATTERN, VALID_TRANSPORTS) -- no magic values
Separation between store (persistence), client manager (connections), and hub (orchestration) follows functional core / imperative shell well

duplicate_details.reason: Same file (mcp-client-manager.ts), same line (44), same category (error-handling)
Same file (mcp-hub.ts), lines 39 vs 40 (within +-3), same category (error-handling)
